home.metaDescription: Discover the history, restoration, and activities of Sint-Geertruikerk in Leuven.

hero.missionBody:
Those who lived before us have gone,
Yet this old house of wood and stone
still stands as a prayer to You.
We pray with them—hear us now.

history.metaDescription: Discover the history of Sint-Geertruikerk and the abbey in Leuven.
history.heroAlt: Interior of Sint-Geertruikerk with woodwork and vaulting
history.heading: History
history.intro: The <abdij>Sint-Geertrui Abbey</abdij> and its surroundings tell a story of centuries of prayer, music, and city life in <leuven>Leuven</leuven>. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Praesent semper feugiat nibh sed pulvinar.
history.section1p1: In the early Middle Ages, <kanunniken>regular canons</kanunniken> formed the core of the religious community. Vestibulum mattis ullamcorper velit sed ullamcorper morbi tincidunt ornare massa.
history.section1p2: Under <hendrik>Henry I of Brabant</hendrik>, the foundation gained greater political and cultural weight. Integer enim neque volutpat ac tincidunt vitae semper quis lectus.
history.section2p1: The bond between the monastery and the urban fabric remained strong for centuries. Nulla facilisi morbi tempus iaculis urna id volutpat lacus laoreet non curabitur.
history.section2p2: Today the ensemble bears witness to late Gothic and Baroque. Arcu cursus vitae congue mauris rhoncus aenean vel elit scelerisque mauris pellentesque.
history.bookTeaserAlt: Open book with a historic photograph of the church

foundation.title: Foundation and church construction
body: Sint-Geertrui was originally the church of the abbey of the same name, founded in 1206 by Duke Henry I of Brabant and his wife Mathilde. The original priory grew into a wealthy and influential community of Augustinian canons regular.

Church and abbey were dedicated to Saint Gertrude of Nivelles. The abbey owned many lands and farms and, within Leuven's 14th-century city walls, also vineyards with an associated wine press house that still exists.

In the shadow of the abbey, Leuven's Small Beguinage emerged, likely as a community of working sisters linked to the abbey. It is certain that the Beguinage remained parish-dependent on the abbey until the 17th century.
foundation.imageAlt: Historic view of the abbey and church

tower.title: Nail-less tower
body: The church was built between the 13th and 15th centuries in Brabantine Gothic style. In 1453 it received its characteristic stone spire, designed by architect Jan van Ruysbroeck. Because the spire is entirely built in natural stone, the church later became known as one of Leuven's Seven Wonders: the church without nails, spikes, or timber.
The church, still in use as a parish church and also used for many musical events, is located on Halfmaartstraat, named after the annual servant market held there under the Ancien Regime.
tower.imageAlt: Detail of the Thierry wing and abbey buildings

thierry.title: Thierry wing
body: The abbey itself, which also played an important political and institutional role in the Duchy of Brabant and for the university founded in 1425, was dissolved by the French revolutionaries who annexed the Southern Netherlands at the end of the 18th century. The buildings were sold and used for various purposes until Canon Armand Thierry founded a new monastery there in the late 19th century and restored the old buildings according to his own vision.
During World War I, the same canon also built a series of unusual facades with fragments from the destruction of many historic buildings by German soldiers in 1914.
Architect Paul Van Aerschot built a row of homes behind it in 1981.

cloister.title: Cloister
body: Today, part of the remaining abbey buildings is in private hands. The church itself belongs to the Sint-Geertrui church board; the rest is owned by the city of Leuven. This also applies to the once majestic cloister, which has largely remained in ruins since May 1944.
The city of Leuven is working on plans to help renovate the entire site, including the majestic former abbot's residence. The site also includes a neo-Gothic chapel used as a scouting museum.
cloister.imageAlt: Historic image of the church after war damage and restoration

bombardment.title: Precision bombing
body: A misdirected British bomb, intended for Leuven's railway marshalling yard, destroyed part of the church and many nearby houses and buildings in May 1944. The church was quickly rebuilt between 1950 and 1953 and resumed service as a Catholic parish church. The building is now in need of major restoration.

It houses, among other things, a carefully restored late-Gothic choir stall, heavily damaged during the bombing but largely returned to its former glory. Notable features also include the expertly restored Penceler organ from 1714 and a remarkable 18th-century Vanden Gheyn carillon, the oldest in Leuven.

This carillon was renovated and expanded in 2025-2026, thanks especially to carillonneur Marc Van Eyck. As resident organist, the church community relies on the renowned Wouter De Koninck, and the instrument is also widely used by students of Leuven's conservatory.

contact.metaDescription: Address, directions, and practical information for visiting Sint-Geertruikerk in Leuven.

hero.imageAlt: Interior of Sint-Geertruikerk with light from the tall windows
hero.title: Contact
hero.lead: Sint-Geertruikerk is in the heart of Leuven. Here you will find our address, how to get here, and tips for your visit.

addressCard.title: Address & directions
addressCard.body: The church is on Halfmaartstraat, near Klein-Begijnhof and the Dijle arms. Public transport and cycling bring you close by; on-street parking nearby is limited. The nearest car park is Parking Vaartkom.

visitCard.title: Visiting
visitCard.p1: Everyone is welcome in the church during services, concerts, or quiet visits whenever the doors are open.
visitCard.p2: See our agenda for concerts, services, and other activities in and around the church.
visitCard.openingHours: Open to the public: in winter on Saturday afternoons from 2 p.m. to 4.30 p.m., and in summer every day from 2 p.m. to 4.30 p.m., except Mondays.

gemeenschapsploeg.title: Community team
gemeenschapsploeg.body: The community team (Gemeenschapsploeg) of the Geertrui community provides pastoral support for what used to be called “the parish”. The Geertrui community is now part of the <zone>Pastoral zone Leuven on the Dijle</zone>, in which all former parishes within the ramparts of the old city work together. The community team leads or supports, for example, Masses and liturgies of the Word in our church.

kerkfabriek.title: Church fabric & council
kerkfabriek.body: The church fabric (kerkfabriek) is a public body responsible for managing the material resources needed to support public worship in a parish. The church council (kerkraad) is the governing body of the fabric — the people who decide on finances, maintenance, and other material matters related to worship. A council typically has five members and may also include a representative of the diocese and several advisors.
kerkfabriek.membersIntro: The Sint-Geertrui church council consists of:

koor.title: Gertrokoor
koor.body:
Together with the church fabric and the community team, the Gertrokoor is simply part of parish life here at Sint-Geertruikerk. For years the choir has been part of Sunday worship — music with heart for the liturgy — under chief conductor Jan Breuls.

Fancy joining? New singers are genuinely welcome. There is usually a rehearsal once a week, often on a Friday evening — ask the choir for the exact time.
koor.imageAlt: The Gertrokoor rehearsing in Sint-Geertruikerk, with chief conductor Jan Breuls and singers in front of the Gothic choir screen.

reachCard.title: Questions about parish matters
reachCard.body: For questions, reservations, or parish matters you can reach us directly.

privacyPage.metaDescription: How we handle personal data on this website.
privacyPage.title: Privacy policy
privacyPage.intro: We respect your privacy. This website provides general information about Sint-Geertruikerk, the agenda, and how to contact us. We do not build marketing profiles and we do not sell personal data.

sections.title: Controller
sections.body: For data protection questions relating to this website, the faith community connected with Sint-Geertruikerk in Leuven is the appropriate contact. For practical matters, please use the contact page or email sintgeertrui@kerkleuven.be.

sections.title: What data?
sections.body:
Simply browsing these pages does not require registration and there is no on-site form that collects personal data by default.

If you voluntarily contact us by email or telephone (as listed on the contact page), we process only the information you provide in order to respond or to handle your request.

sections.title: Purposes and legal basis
sections.body: We use data only to inform you or to answer your request. Where technical delivery of the website requires processing (for example standard server logs at the hosting provider), this may also rely on a legitimate interest in operating the site securely and reliably.

sections.title: Retention
sections.body: We do not maintain a database of visitor behaviour for advertising. Information you send us directly is kept no longer than necessary to handle your enquiry.

sections.title: Your rights
sections.body:
Where applicable privacy law grants them, you may exercise rights of access, rectification or erasure, restriction of processing, and objection.

You may also lodge a complaint with the Belgian Data Protection Authority (Drukpersstraat 35, 1000 Brussels, www.dataprotectionauthority.be).

sections.title: Cookies
sections.body: For information about cookies, please see the separate Cookies page on this website.

sections.title: Changes
sections.body: This statement may be updated when the website or the law changes. Please review it from time to time.

privacyPage.footnote: This text is general information and does not constitute individual legal advice.

cookiesPage.metaDescription: How this website uses cookies and similar technologies.
cookiesPage.title: Cookies
cookiesPage.intro: This page describes in general terms how cookies and similar technologies may occur in connection with this website. We do not use our own tracking cookies for advertising.

sections.title: What are cookies?
sections.body: Cookies are small text files that may be placed on your device when you visit a website. They are often used to make a site work correctly or to remember basic preferences.

sections.title: What this site uses
sections.body:
We do not set analytics or advertising cookies to measure or sell your browsing behaviour.

The technical software behind the website may use strictly necessary session or security cookies or similar storage to deliver the pages safely; these are typically limited in duration and purpose.

sections.title: Third-party content
sections.body: Where a page embeds a video player from YouTube, that service may use its own cookies or similar technologies when you start or view the content. Google's privacy and cookie policies apply.

sections.title: Your choices
sections.body: You can configure your browser to refuse or delete cookies. Some website features may then work less smoothly.

sections.title: Changes
sections.body: This page may be updated if the website or embedded services change.

cookiesPage.footnote: This text is general information and does not constitute individual legal advice.

restoration.title: Restoration of Sint-Geertrui church
restoration.intro: In May 1944, Sint-Geertrui church was hit by a stray British bomb intended for Leuven's railway marshalling yard. The damage was significant. The church was quickly rebuilt, but 75 years later it once again needs serious restoration.

spire.title: Spire
spire.body: The unique spire (the nail-less tower and one of Leuven's so-called Wonders) was already thoroughly restored more than twenty years ago.

towerBody.title: Tower body
towerBody.body: In 2024, small fragments were found falling from the underlying tower body. For safety reasons, debris nets were installed. In agreement with the city, the Sint-Geertrui church board - the institution responsible for the church building among other things - is planning restoration in the coming years, before Leuven becomes European Capital of Culture in 2030.
towerBody.imageAlt: Restoration image of the tower body

humidity.title: Moisture
humidity.body: Stability and rising damp: the entire church building must be protected against rising moisture, especially in the outer walls. Many exterior windows also need repair and reinforcement. Leuven is a wet area, crossed by waterways, primarily branches of the Dijle that flow past the church.
humidity.imageAlt: Restoration image related to moisture issues

heatingStability.title: Heating and stability
heatingStability.body: Heating: the church has not been heated for decades, except during services with terrace heaters. With an unexpected subsidy from the Flemish government, the church board now wants to install minimal heating in the building, hoping to use alternative energy in a later phase, ideally for the wider neighborhood. Parts of the attic will also be insulated. This restoration phase starts in October 2026 and will likely last six months. Two rear pillars in the choir will also be reinforced against subsidence during this phase.
heatingStability.imageAlt: Restoration image of heating and stability works

roof.title: Roof
roof.body: The church roof needs replacement. In agreement with the city, works were carried out at the end of 2025 to make the roof watertight. Once watertight, insulation can be placed on the vaults, reducing roughly half of the church's heat loss. These insulation works will take place in 2026-2027 together with installation of a heating system.

carillon.title: Carillon
carillon.body: The carillon was under restoration in mid-2005. By the end of that year, works were expected to be completed and a new octave added. It is the oldest carillon in the city, which has four others. The core of the Vanden Gheyn carillon in Sint-Geertrui church dates back to the 18th century.
carillon.videoIframeTitle: Embedded YouTube video about the carillon of Sint-Geertruikerk

outro.title: Interior
outro.body: The interior of the church also needs attention. The most urgent area is the Chapel of the Seven Sorrows, on the right when entering through the portal. In the longer term, the church board also wants to restore the connection to the abbey wing that is still largely in ruins.
